Rationale: The intervention educated patients and provided them with a provider prompt to encourage them to ask for colorectal cancer screening, which fits the definition of increasing demand for a clinical innovation.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Participants in the standard condition were mailed a FIT and received live telephone reminders to return it. Participants in the enhanced condition also received a tailored advance notiﬁcation (text message or live phone call) and two automated phone call reminders.
Rationale: The study intervention explicitly used advance notifications and various types of reminders (live phone call, automated phone call, text message) to encourage patients to complete and return their mailed fecal immunochemical test kits.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Group 1 patients were delivered the following components: (i) an advance notiﬁcation one-way text message (1–2 days before anticipated receipt of the mailed FIT); (ii) a mailed FIT test; (iii) a series of two automated phone call reminders (sent 2 weeks and 4 weeks following the mailing); and (iv) live reminder calls delivered at the discretion of clinic staff on care teams (beginning six weeks following the mailing), consistent with usual care.
Rationale: The study implemented an intervention that included advance text message notifications and a series of automated and live phone call reminders to encourage patients to complete the mailed FIT test, which directly aligns with the strategy of intervening with patients to enhance uptake.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Each week, a member of the study team reviewed each participant’s data with them, praising achievements, identifying barriers to physical activity, and discussing solutions.
Rationale: This describes a deliberate strategy of reviewing patient-generated data and problem-solving with them to encourage adherence to the physical activity component of the intervention, which directly fits the definition.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Patient navigators assisted patients with a myriad of services including CRC screening education, barrier assessment, addressing barriers to completing a colonoscopy, language translation, appointment reminders and scheduling, acting as a liaison with the gastroenterology facility, bowel prep education assistance, and transportation and companion assistance.
Rationale: The patient navigation program directly intervened with patients through reminders, scheduling assistance, and problem-solving around barriers to encourage adherence to the recommended follow-up colonoscopy.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Staff also placed CCS clinic reminder calls 1 d before the scheduled CCS-only clinic.
Rationale: The intervention included placing reminder calls to patients one day before their scheduled appointment, which is a specific example of intervening with patients to enhance uptake as defined by the strategy.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: As part of this tailored intervention, the patient navigator provided reminder calls prior to colonoscopy procedures at 1 week, 2 days (for prep), and 1 day before to avoid no-shows and last-minute cancellations.
Rationale: The use of patient navigators to provide reminder calls and follow-up phone calls to ensure screening completion is a direct application of intervening with patients to enhance uptake and adherence.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Brief monthly calls were conducted with families during the delay period as a retention strategy.
Rationale: The use of brief monthly calls is a deliberate strategy to keep families engaged and in the study, which directly supports adherence to the study protocol and intervention.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: If the FIT kit was not returned in two weeks (the timing suggested by community members and supported by research), a mailed reminder letter to complete the FIT was sent to patients in the video brochure group.
Rationale: The study used mailed reminder letters to prompt patients who had not returned their FIT kit, which is a direct intervention with patients to enhance uptake of the screening test.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: sent 73 outreach messages speciﬁc to scheduling appointments for patients to obtain cervical cancer screening.
Rationale: The project sent outreach messages to patients, which function as reminders to schedule appointments and enhance uptake of the screening service, directly aligning with the strategy's definition.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: The weekly 2 h sessions were broken down into the following seven segments: (S1) assessment of personal goal; (S2) review of last week’s topic; (S3) review of weekly goal; (S4) introduction to the week’s topic; (S5) lecture and activities; (S6) physical activity; and (S7) summary of the session.
Rationale: The intervention's weekly structure, which included assessing and reviewing personal goals, is a direct strategy with patients to encourage and problem-solve around adherence to the program's behavioral targets.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Follow-up calls 1–3 (optional 4th call) Check-in on goal progress (successes and challenges related to the topic(s) covered) Check-in on referrals (actions taken or barriers to following through or receiving services) Reminder or scheduling of next counseling session
Rationale: The study used follow-up calls to check on participants' progress, problem-solve challenges, and discuss barriers, which directly fits the definition of intervening with patients to enhance uptake and adherence.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Call patient to provide reminders, identify barriers, and solutions for scheduling

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence

Rationale: The text describes a patient navigation program that used text message reminders, reminder calls, and motivational interviewing to encourage patients to return their colorectal cancer screening kits, which directly aligns with the definition of intervening with patients to enhance uptake and adherence.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: Follow-up visits were scheduled according to the patient’s HbA1c levels. For patients with HbA1c levels greater than 9%, follow-up visits were recommended every 2 weeks. In contrast, patients with HbA1c levels between 6% and 8% (relatively stable glucose control) were scheduled for follow-up every 3 months.
Rationale: This describes a structured system of follow-up visits, tailored to patient need, to review data and manage the intervention, which is a strategy to encourage and problem-solve around adherence.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: The project navigator called all participants one and two weeks after the packets were sent to remind them to return their FIT kits and forms.

Strategy name: Intervene with patients/consumers to enhance uptake and adherence
Textual Evidence: In addition, 18 mentioned liking logistic functions of the SMS text messaging program, such as the texts acting as reminders and the quiz functions. Rationale: The study involved patients through a community engagement process called boot camp translation to develop and refine the content and format of the intervention materials, directly fitting the definition of engaging consumers in the implementation effort.

Discussions centered on barriers and facilitators for CRC screening and the content and format for the proposed intervention materials.
Rationale: The intervention materials were developed based on extensive input from community members through focus groups and interviews, directly involving them in the design of the implementation effort.

Textual Evidence: When asked what aspects they would change about the SMS text messaging program, 29 of 43 (67%) patients reported that they would change nothing, 6 (14%) wanted more information or content, 5 (12%) wanted the ability to respond to more SMS text messages, and 3 (7%) wanted to change the timing of the messages.
Rationale: The study collected feedback from patients via free-text questions about their experience with the text messaging program and what they would change, which constitutes involving patients in the evaluation of the implementation effort.

Strategy name: Prepare patients/consumers to be active participants
Textual Evidence: Patient navigators assisted patients with a myriad of services including CRC screening education, barrier assessment, addressing barriers to completing a colonoscopy, language translation, appointment reminders and scheduling, acting as a liaison with the gastroenterology facility, bowel prep education assistance, and transportation and companion assistance.

Strategy name: Prepare patients/consumers to be active participants
Textual Evidence: Upon completing the TIMI, participants received a printed provider prompt indicating their intent regarding CRCS: (1) “I will definitely get screened to prevent CRC”, (2) “I am thinking about getting screened for CRC”, or (3) “I am not interested in getting screened for CRC.”).
Rationale: The intervention used a provider prompt to help patients articulate their screening intentions and facilitate a discussion with their doctor, thereby preparing them to be active participants in their care.

Strategy name: Prepare patients/consumers to be active participants
Textual Evidence: The final HRA captured baseline and follow-up chronic disease behaviors, patient knowledge and attitudes, and chronic disease self-management.
Rationale: The program used a Health Risk Assessment to explicitly measure and address patient knowledge, attitudes, and self-management skills, with the goal of increasing their confidence and ability to manage their own chronic conditions, which prepares them to be active participants in their care.

Strategy name: Prepare patients/consumers to be active participants
Textual Evidence: The 13-week calendar presented topics in the following order: (W1) introduction to MSD; (W2) living a healthy life with T2D; (W3) are you at risk of developing CVD?; (W4) maintaining a healthy weight; (W5) beneﬁts of physical activity; (W6) glucose and sugar; (W7) everything you need to know about high blood pressure, salt, and sodium; (W8) control your cholesterol, eat less fat; (W9) is our community healthy?; (W10) enjoy life with emotional well-being; (W11) effective management of T2D is a shared responsibility; (W12) enjoy healthy meals with friends and family, meal demonstration; and (W13) review/graduation.
Rationale: The intervention curriculum included a specific session on 'effective management of T2D is a shared responsibility,' which directly prepares patients to be active participants in their own care.

Strategy name: Prepare patients/consumers to be active participants
Textual Evidence: AltaMed provided informational brochures to educate patients on all available screening options, and patients could choose a CRC screening test, for example FIT or FIT‐DNA, based on their preference during their discussion with providers.
Rationale: The program provided informational brochures on all screening options and allowed patients to choose their preferred screening test in discussion with their provider, which prepares patients to be active participants in their care decisions.

Strategy name: Prepare patients/consumers to be active participants

Textual Evidence: Patients in the colonoscopy-only arm were mailed a letter describing the importance of colorectal cancer screening and encouraging them to get screened via colonoscopy using the colonoscopy referral included with the letter.

Rationale: The study used mass media channels, specifically a listserv, e-newsletter, and mass mailings, to recruit a large number of health centers to participate in the implementation of the intervention.

Textual Evidence: Project ECHO Diabetes addresses this issue by using a "hub-and-spoke" model to deliver tele-education, empowering primary care providers to manage diabetes care effectively in these settings.

Textual Evidence: Boxes could be home-delivered (by food bank staff) or kept on-site at the food bank for patient pick-up, based on patient needs. Most patients chose to have food boxes delivered to their homes.
Rationale: The program changed the location of service delivery by offering home delivery of grocery boxes, which increased patient access by mitigating transportation barriers.

Textual Evidence: FIT can be completed at home and returned by mail, making mailed FIT outreach programs a promising way to reach people and increase CRC screening in rural geographic regions like Appalachia (e.g., mostly rural with lower access to healthcare, lack of public transportation8–10).
Rationale: The study implements a mailed FIT outreach program, which changes the site of the screening service from the clinic to the patient's home to increase access for a rural population.

Textual Evidence: The intervention consisted of connected blood glucose and blood pressure monitors, a data-enabled tablet, virtual health coaching sessions twice per month, and virtual or in-person provider visits as needed.
Rationale: This intervention changed the site of clinical service by using remote patient monitoring and virtual coaching to deliver care in patients' homes, thereby increasing access.

Textual Evidence: Patients in the FIT-only arm were mailed a letter describing the importance of colorectal cancer screening and encouraging them to get screened using the enclosed FIT kit.
Rationale: The study mailed fecal immunochemical test (FIT) kits directly to patients' homes, which constitutes bringing the clinical service to the client's home to increase access to screening.

Textual Evidence: Patients were prescribed a weekly food voucher to be redeemed at the Merced County Food Bank.

Textual Evidence: AltaMed also used mass mailers with FIT kits to reach patients before 2021 and continued this practice with the patient navigation program.
Rationale: The strategy of mailing FIT kits directly to patients' homes changes the location of the service from the clinic to the patient's home, which aligns with the definition of changing service sites to increase access.

Textual Evidence: This paper describes the impact of an additional navigation activity, such as using introductory calls before sending mailed in-home FIT kits, on CRC screening completion in two federally qualified health centers (FQHCs) in a U.S.–Mexico border county.
Rationale: The program implemented mailed in-home FIT kits, which changes the site of the screening service from the clinic to the patient's home to increase access by overcoming barriers like transportation.
